title: Rustic hazel twig frame
subtitle: A picture or mirror frame made from hazel twigs and small branches
excerpt: A rustic frame for a small print or mirror, built from paired hazel twigs lashed at the corners and decorated with shorter cross-twigs. The finished frame has an inner opening of 150 mm by 200 mm. Green hazel bends slightly at the corner lashings; the frame dries and stiffens in the finished shape. No carpentry tools required.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the twig frame construction method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the rustic decorative frame form.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently cut wood, still high in moisture content. Green hazel twigs are flexible enough to bend at corner lashings; they stiffen as they dry.
A binding of thin cord or withy that holds two or more pieces together at a junction. The corner lashings are the main joint in this frame.
A 45-degree diagonal cut at a corner. Here, the twig ends are cut at 45 degrees where they meet at the corners of the frame.

content.text: A twig frame uses the natural form and texture of
green

hazel twigs rather than trying to suppress them. The paired twig construction means small variations in twig diameter or straightness add to rather than detract from the finished look. The frame is held at the corners by
lashings
of thin hazel withy or strong waxed cord; no glue is needed.
Wood

Fresh-cut hazel twigs from coppice or hedgerow, 8 to 12 mm in diameter. Straight sections work best for the main frame; curved or forked pieces can be used as decorative cross-pieces. The four frame sides need twig lengths of: two at 320 mm (for the top and bottom) and two at 260 mm (for the sides), each giving the inner opening of 200 mm by 150 mm plus the twig diameter overhang at each corner. Collect more than needed; select the straightest four for the frame.
Tools
Secateurs: for cutting twigs to length.
Sloyd knife: for paring the corner mitre cuts and trimming cross-piece ends.
Cordless drill: for the hanging hole in the back piece.
Leather strop.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Cutting and preparing the frame pieces
Cut the four main twigs to length with the secateurs. Pare a
mitre

cut at each corner end with the sloyd knife: a 45-degree diagonal that brings the twig ends together at each corner rather than overlapping them. The mitre is not essential but gives a neater corner and allows a tighter lashing. Remove the bark from the mitre face only; leave the rest of the bark intact.
Lashing the corners

Hold two twig ends together at a corner and wrap them tightly with waxed cord or a thin hazel withy: six to eight tight turns, then a half-hitch to lock. Work each corner in the same way. The green hazel twigs can be slightly flexed to sit flat against each other at the mitre; once the lashing dries, the joint is rigid.
Adding cross-pieces

Lay short cross-pieces of 4 to 6 mm hazel twig across the frame at irregular intervals, cutting them to length with secateurs. Lash each one at both contact points. Forked twigs, small sprigs, or curved pieces add visual interest.
Hanging and drying

Hang the frame upright for two to three weeks to dry. The lashings tighten as the twigs shrink slightly on drying. Bore a small hanging hole in the top lashing after drying and thread a cord through for wall mounting. The frame is ready to accept a print or photograph mounted on a backing board and held in the frame with small twig wedges or picture clips.
